questions: What GitHub mechanism should host the prebuilt artifact — GitHub Releases (permanent assets) or GitHub Actions artifacts (which expire)?
What should the prebuilt tarball contain and how should the CI workflow produce it?
How should the updater discover the remote commit SHA without requiring git on the user's machine?
How should the staged download-validate-swap mechanism work, including install-root discovery and atomic swap?
How should the updater handle authentication for private repository assets and produce explicit error messages?
How should the updater extract the tarball on the user's machine without external dependencies?
What is the risk of file-locking on Windows during the swap step?

answers.answer: GitHub Releases with a rolling 'latest' tag is the correct mechanism. Release assets are permanent (no expiration, unlike Actions artifacts which expire in 90 days by default). The tag 'latest' does NOT match the 'v*' pattern in publish.yml, so it will not trigger npm publish. GITHUB_TOKEN with 'contents: write' permission can create and manage releases via 'gh release create' — no custom RELEASE_TOKEN needed. Release assets have stable download URLs via both browser_download_url (public) and the API endpoint (private/public with auth). Actions artifacts were rejected: they expire, require API auth for all downloads, and the retention window makes them unsuitable as a permanent update channel.
evidence: publish.yml:5-6 — trigger pattern 'v*' will not match tag 'latest'
Context7 GitHub Actions docs — confirmed GITHUB_TOKEN with 'contents: write' can create releases
Context7 GitHub Actions docs — permissions key supports 'contents: write' for release creation
GitHub Actions artifact default retention is 90 days — too short for a permanent update channel

answers.answer: The tarball should contain: dist/ (excluding test files), skill-content/, package.json, and a new build-metadata.json with the commit SHA and build timestamp. This matches the existing npm 'files' array (package.json:24-29). The project has zero production dependencies (only devDependencies: @types/node and typescript), so no node_modules/ is needed. The CI workflow creates the tarball after running 'npm ci', 'npm test', then uses 'tar -czf' with test file exclusions. build-metadata.json enables the updater to read the commit SHA from the installed artifact without a network call.
evidence: package.json:24-29 — files: ['dist', '!dist/**/*.test.js', '!dist/**/*.test.d.ts', 'skill-content']
package.json:40-43 — only devDependencies, no production dependencies
.npmignore — excludes dist/**/*.test.js and dist/**/*.test.d.ts
publish.yml:50-52 — existing tarball validation checks for dist/index.js, package.json, skill-content/SKILL.md

answers.answer: Replace 'git ls-remote' with a single GitHub REST API call to the releases endpoint: GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/releases/tags/latest. This returns the release object including target_commitish (the commit SHA), plus the assets array with download URLs. One API call provides both the SHA for comparison AND the asset download URL — eliminating the git binary dependency. Rate limits are manageable: 60 requests/hour unauthenticated, 5000/hour with a token. The updater only makes this call when a user runs 'hlx update' or during periodic auto-update checks.
evidence: src/update/check.ts:18-19 — current fetchRemoteSha() uses 'git ls-remote', requiring git on user machine
package.json:22 — engines.node >= 18, so native fetch() is available for HTTP calls
GitHub REST API: GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/releases/tags/{tag} returns release with target_commitish and assets[]

answers.answer: The staged download-validate-swap mechanism works in 5 phases: (1) DISCOVER: Query the GitHub Releases API for the 'latest' release, extract commit SHA and asset URL. Compare remote SHA to local SHA from config. (2) DOWNLOAD: Fetch the tarball to ~/.hlx/staging/<sha>.tgz using Node.js native fetch(). (3) UNPACK+VALIDATE: Extract to ~/.hlx/staging/<sha>/, verify dist/index.js exists, run 'node <staged>/dist/index.js --version' with HLX_SKIP_UPDATE_CHECK=1. (4) SWAP: Determine the live install root via import.meta.url (resolves to dist/update/*.js, so ../../ is the package root). Rename live dist/ → dist.bak/, staged dist/ → live dist/. Repeat for skill-content/ and package.json. If any rename fails, restore from .bak. (5) RECORD: Update installSource in ~/.hlx/config.json with mode='github', commit=<sha>. Clean up staging and .bak directories. Install-root discovery via import.meta.url is already proven in the codebase (version.ts:18, paths.ts:18).
evidence: src/update/version.ts:18 — dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)) resolves to dist/update/
src/skill/paths.ts:18 — same import.meta.url pattern for package root resolution
src/lib/config.ts:37-38 — CONFIG_DIR = ~/.hlx/, existing staging dir would be adjacent
src/lib/config.ts:201-212 — saveConfig() uses read-merge-write, safe for updating installSource
src/update/validate.ts:59-64 — existing --version validation pattern with HLX_SKIP_UPDATE_CHECK=1

answers.answer: Auth discovery follows the standard GitHub token chain: (1) GITHUB_TOKEN env var, (2) GH_TOKEN env var, (3) 'gh auth token' command via child_process (if gh CLI is installed). For public repos, no auth is needed for the Releases API or asset downloads. For private repos, the asset download URL must use the API endpoint (api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/releases/assets/{id}) with 'Accept: application/octet-stream' and 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' headers — the browser_download_url does not work without browser cookies. Error messaging: HTTP 404 → 'No update release found. Ensure a CI build has completed.'; HTTP 401/403 → 'GitHub authentication required. Set GITHUB_TOKEN env var or run gh auth login.'; Network error → 'Could not reach GitHub. Check your network connection.' The updater should attempt unauthenticated first, then authenticated if 404/403 is returned (to handle both public and private repos gracefully).
evidence: Ticket requirement: 'If the GitHub repo/artifact is private and the user is not authenticated to GitHub, the updater must fail with a clear message telling the user exactly what kind of GitHub auth is expected.'
GitHub REST API docs: Private release assets require authenticated API download with Accept: application/octet-stream header
src/update/check.ts:3-6 — CANONICAL_REPO_URL and CANONICAL_REPO constants already define the GitHub repo coordinates

answers.answer: Use the system 'tar' command via child_process.execSync for tarball extraction. The 'tar' command is available on: macOS (built-in), Linux (built-in), and Windows 10+ (built-in since build 17063/Dec 2017). Since the project requires Node >= 18, users will overwhelmingly be on OS versions that include tar. This avoids adding npm dependencies (like 'tar' package) to a project with zero production dependencies. The extraction command: 'tar -xzf <tarball> -C <staging-dir>'. If tar is unavailable (unlikely edge case), the error surfaces clearly as a spawn failure.
evidence: package.json:22 — engines.node >= 18, implying modern OS
package.json:40-43 — zero production dependencies; adding a tar library would break this property
Windows 10 tar availability: shipped since build 17063 (December 2017)
src/update/perform.ts — existing code already uses child_process.spawnSync for external commands

answers.answer: File-locking risk on Windows during swap is LOW for this specific case. Node.js reads module files into V8's heap during import and then closes the file handle — it does not hold open file locks on .js files after loading. The update command is short-lived: it downloads, validates, swaps, and exits. The NEXT invocation loads the new files. The rename-based swap (dist/ → dist.bak/, staged/ → dist/) operates on directories, which Windows allows when no file handles are open inside them. The only risk is if another process (antivirus scanner, editor indexer) has an open handle on a file inside dist/. Mitigation: if the rename fails, the updater retries once after a short delay, then reports the failure with guidance to close other programs and retry. The backup directory (.bak) provides a clean rollback path.
evidence: Node.js ESM loader: files are read and parsed during import; handles are closed after read
src/update/perform.ts:22-23 — existing code sets env vars on spawned child processes, showing child_process is the execution model
Ticket requirement: 'If unpacking fails, hlx update must exit non-zero and keep the current install unchanged' — backup dirs enable this

statement: The update mechanism should use GitHub Releases with a rolling 'latest' tag (not matching 'v*'), built by a new CI workflow (build-release.yml) using GITHUB_TOKEN with contents:write. The updater replaces git ls-remote with a single GitHub REST API call to GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/releases/tags/latest, which provides both the commit SHA and asset download URL. The staged install mechanism downloads the tarball to ~/.hlx/staging/, validates via entrypoint existence and --version check, then does a rename-based swap with backup directories for rollback. Install-root discovery uses the proven import.meta.url pattern already in the codebase. Auth follows the standard GITHUB_TOKEN → GH_TOKEN → gh auth token chain with explicit error messaging. The tarball contains dist/, skill-content/, package.json, and build-metadata.json — zero production dependencies means no node_modules needed.
